chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Why Einstein's equation explains the photoelectric effect
summary: Einstein's equation KE_max = hν − φ₀ says a photon's energy frees an electron (overcoming the work function φ₀) and gives it kinetic energy — explaining the threshold and the role of frequency.
body:
**Must know**
- $KE_{max}=h\nu-\phi_0$, where the work function $\phi_0=h\nu_0$.
- In terms of stopping potential: $eV_0=h\nu-\phi_0$.
- Below $\nu_0$ no electron is emitted (the photon energy is less than $\phi_0$).
- A graph of $V_0$ versus $\nu$ is a straight line of slope $h/e$.
**Exam traps**
- One photon ejects at most one electron.
- $KE_{max}$ is linear in frequency; its intercept gives the work function.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: How experiments reveal the laws of the photoelectric effect
summary: Photoelectric experiments show emission is instantaneous, the stopping potential depends on frequency (not intensity), and there is a threshold frequency below which no emission occurs.
body:
**Must know**
- The saturation photocurrent is proportional to the light intensity (above threshold).
- The stopping potential $V_0$ satisfies $eV_0=KE_{max}$ and depends on frequency, not intensity.
- There is a threshold frequency $\nu_0$ below which no emission occurs, whatever the intensity.
**Exam traps**
- The stopping potential is independent of intensity; only the saturation current scales with intensity.
- Emission is essentially instantaneous (~10⁻⁹ s), not delayed.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Why both light and matter show wave–particle duality
summary: Light shows wave behaviour (interference, diffraction) and particle behaviour (the photoelectric effect); matter likewise shows both — duality is universal.
body:
**Must know**
- Light: wave aspect (interference, diffraction) and particle aspect (photon, photoelectric and Compton effects).
- Matter: particle aspect and wave aspect (de Broglie waves, electron diffraction).
- Which aspect appears depends on the experiment performed.
**Exam traps**
- Wave and particle pictures are complementary, not contradictory.
- Planck's constant $h$ is the bridge between the two descriptions.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Objective I
summary: The photoelectric effect (photoemission of electrons when light hits a metal) cannot be explained by classical wave theory — the effect depends on **frequency** (not intensity) of light, and has no time lag.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- The photoelectric effect (photoemission of electrons when light hits a metal) cannot be explained by classical wave theory — the effect depends on **frequency** (not intensity) of light, and has no time lag. Einstein (1905) explained it by treating light as **photons** (particles of energy $E = h
u$). The photoelectric effect is direct evidence of the **particle nature** of light. Answer: (B).
- Einstein's photoelectric equation: $$KE_{m max} = h
u - \phi$$ where $h
u$ is the photon energy and $\phi = h
u_0$ is the work function (minimum energy to eject an electron). $KE_{m max}$ depends on **frequency** (not intensity) and is zero at the threshold frequency $
u_0 = \phi/h$. Increasing intensity increases the number of photoelectrons but not their maximum KE. Answer: (B).
- The **stopping potential** $V_s$ is the minimum reverse (negative) voltage applied to the anode that brings the photocurrent to zero. Even the fastest photoelectrons (with maximum KE) are stopped: $$eV_s = KE_{m max} = h
u - \phi$$ Measuring $V_s$ is the standard way to determine $KE_{m max}$ and hence test Einstein's equation. Answer: (B).
- De Broglie's relation: $\lambda = h/p$ where $p$ is momentum. For kinetic energy $K = p^2/(2m)$: $p = \sqrt{2mK}$. $$\lambda =  rac{h}{p} =  rac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$$ This describes the wave nature of matter — confirmed by electron diffraction experiments. Answer: (B).
- No matter how intense the light, if the photon energy $h
u < \phi$ (work function), no single photon has enough energy to eject an electron. Emission does not occur below the **threshold frequency** $
u_0 = \phi/h$. Low intensity only reduces the number of photoelectrons (not whether emission occurs). Answer: (B).

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Objective II
summary: Classical wave theory predicts: - Any frequency should work if intensity is high enough (wrong → A).
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Classical wave theory predicts: - Any frequency should work if intensity is high enough (wrong → A). - Energy accumulation would require a time lag (wrong → B). - Stopping potential should depend on intensity (wrong → C). The fact that photocurrent increases with intensity (D) is actually consistent with quantum theory too (more photons → more electrons), but it doesn't contradict classical theory. A, B, C are the hallmarks that classical theory fails to explain. Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- De Broglie (1924) proposed that **all matter** has an associated wavelength $\lambda = h/p$. This has been confirmed experimentally for: - **Electrons** (Davisson–Germer diffraction experiment ✓) - **Protons** and **neutrons** (neutron diffraction used in crystallography ✓) - **Photons** (wave: interference/diffraction; particle: photoelectric effect ✓) Wave-particle duality is a universal quantum property. Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D).

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: How Planck's constant unifies radiation and matter
summary: Planck's constant h links a photon's energy to its frequency (E = hν) and a particle's wavelength to its momentum (λ = h/p), underpinning both the photoelectric effect and matter waves.
body:
**Must know**
- Photon: $E=h\nu$, $p=\dfrac{h}{\lambda}$.
- Photoelectric effect: $KE_{max}=h\nu-\phi_0$.
- Matter wave: $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}$.
**Exam traps**
- Distinguish the work function from the threshold frequency ($\phi_0=h\nu_0$).
- $KE_{max}$ is linear in frequency, not in intensity.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: How light behaves as a stream of photons
summary: Light comes in quanta called photons, each carrying energy E = hν = hc/λ and momentum p = h/λ; photons are massless and travel at speed c.
body:
**Must know**
- Photon energy: $E=h\nu=\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.
- Photon momentum: $p=\dfrac{h}{\lambda}=\dfrac{E}{c}$.
- A photon has zero rest mass and always moves at $c$; $h=6.63\times10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$.
**Exam traps**
- Photon energy depends on frequency, not on intensity; intensity $=$ (photons per second) $\times h\nu$.
- Photon momentum is $p=E/c$, not $mv$.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: How every moving particle has a de Broglie wavelength
summary: Every moving particle has an associated wavelength λ = h/p = h/mv (de Broglie); this wave nature matters only for very small particles such as electrons.
body:
**Must know**
- de Broglie wavelength: $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{p}=\dfrac{h}{mv}$.
- In terms of kinetic energy: $\lambda=\dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$.
- For an electron accelerated through $V$ volts: $\lambda=\dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{V}}\ \text{\AA}$.
- Confirmed experimentally by Davisson–Germer electron diffraction.
**Exam traps**
- $\lambda\propto 1/p$: a heavier or faster particle has a shorter wavelength.
- Macroscopic objects have an utterly negligible de Broglie wavelength.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: reNEET2026
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Photocurrent $\propto$ intensity (power), so $I$ falls linearly. Stopping potential depends only on the photon frequency, which is unchanged, so $|V|$ stays constant.
- $\lambda_1<\lambda\Rightarrow$ no emission, $V_1=0$. Stopping potential grows with threshold wavelength, and $\lambda_3\gg\lambda_2$, so $V_3>V_2$, i.e. $V_2<V_3.$
- $p_e=\sqrt{2mE}$, $p_{ph}=E/c\Rightarrow\dfrac{p_e}{p_{ph}}=c\sqrt{\dfrac{2m}{E}}=3\times10^8\sqrt{\dfrac{2(9\times10^{-31})}{3.2\times10^{-18}}}\approx225.$
